title: Non-Abelian Particle-Loop, Fracton, and Planon Condensation in Cage-Net Models
authors: Yifei Wang, Yu Zhao, Yingcheng Li, Hao Song, Yidun Wan
abstract: We present a framework for non-Abelian p-loop, fracton, and planon condensation in 3+1 dimensions by constructing extended cage-net fracton models using decoupled layers of the Hu-Geer-Wu (HGW) string-net model. These cage-net models extend the conventional cage-net models based on the Levin-Wen (LW) string-net model in the sense that they inherit the tail degrees of freedom of the HGW models, which are essential for completely describing the internal spaces of quasiparticles. This approach allows us to explicitly derive the quasiparticle spectra of the cage-net models by projecting those of the parent 2D HGW layers. Utilizing this framework, we can condense the p-loops formed by non-Abelian anyons within a fracton phase. Specifically, we construct the condensation projector for $(σ\barσ, 1)$-loops within the extended Ising Cage-Net (ICN) model. We demonstrate that condensing these non-Abelian loops drives a phase transition that maps the ICN model to the X-cube (XC) model defined on a truncated cubic lattice, a process that explicitly reveals the splitting of non-Abelian planons into distinct sub-dimensional excitations. Furthermore, our framework extends to the condensation of fractons and planons: we demonstrate that in the ICN model fracton condensation drives the decoupling of the 3D fracton order back into isolated 2D topological order layers, while planon condensation collapses the system entirely into a trivial phase. Our results establish a concrete Hamiltonian mechanism for phase transitions between distinct fracton orders and provide a generalizable method for analyzing the evolution of sub-dimensional excitations.
summary: This paper presents a new framework for studying phase transitions between different fracton orders in 3+1 dimensions. By using the Hu-Geer-Wu string-net model as a parent, the authors show how condensing non-Abelian loops can drive transitions between complex phases like the extended Ising Cage-Net and the X-cube model. The work explicitly tracks how quasiparticles like planons split or become confined during these transitions. This establishes a generalizable method for analyzing the evolution of sub-dimensional excitations in higher-dimensional topological phases.
main_problem: The paper addresses the lack of established Hamiltonian mechanisms for phase transitions between different non-trivial fracton orders and the difficulty of formulating condensation operators for non-Abelian anyons in 3D.
main_result: The authors demonstrate a concrete mechanism for phase transitions between distinct fracton orders, specifically showing that condensing non-Abelian p-loops can map an extended Ising Cage-Net model to the X-cube model.
method: The study uses a framework of anyon and p-loop condensation applied to decoupled layers of the Hu-Geer-Wu (HGW) string-net model, employing algebraic tools like Unitary Fusion Categories and Hamiltonian engineering via condensation projectors.
model_or_system: The system consists of 3+1 dimensional cage-net models constructed from decoupled 2D HGW string-net layers, including the Extended Ising Cage-Net (ICN) model and the X-cube (XC) model on a truncated cubic lattice.
key_observables: Quasiparticle spectra (fractons, lineons, and planons), anyon dynamics (splitting, identification, and confinement), and topological spin/braiding statistics.
important_parameters: The infinite coupling limit of the condensation term (Lambda to infinity) and the specific fusion rules of the Ising Unitary Fusion Category.
main_assumptions: The framework focuses on the ICN model as a specific showcase; it also assumes that certain non-condensable fusion products (like fermions) limit the full condensation of non-Abelian p-loops.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the HGW lattice with tails; Figure 2 shows the 2D truncated square lattice geometry; Figure 3 depicts the 3D truncated cubic lattice; Figure 4 illustrates the local components of the p-loop projector; Figure 5 shows the p-loop condensate configuration.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the HGW model and its tail degrees of freedom, constructs the 3D extended cage-net models via p-loop condensation, derives the effective Hamiltonian for the ICN and X-cube models, and analyzes the resulting quasiparticle spectra and phase transition dynamics.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous mathematical framework for understanding how complex, sub-dimensional excitations like fractons and planons emerge and evolve during topological phase transitions, which is fundamental to many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Part of the HGW model lattice. A tail (wavy line) is attached to an arbitrary edge of every plaquette.

Figure 2: Configuration of the HGW model on the 2-dimensional truncated square lattice. The lattice geometry is constructed from trivalent vertices and features two distinct types of plaquettes: large octagonal plaquettes and smaller square (or diamond-like) plaquettes. A tail degree of freedom, depicted as a wavy line, is attached to every plaquette of both types and points inward, satisfying the requirements for the HGW formalism in this modified geometry.

Figure 3: 3D truncated cubic lattice formed by intersecting three orthogonal stacks of 2D HGW layers. The layers are color-coded according to their orientation: green, red, and blue shading indicates layers normal to the x, y, and z directions, respectively. The lattice structure features principal edges which are formed at the intersection of two perpendicular layers and carry a pair of labels (jE, kE ∈F). A tail (wavy line) is specifically attached to each principal edge and directed into the adjacent octagonal plaquette. This configuration ensures that every octagonal plaquette possesses exactly one tail , essential for defining the dyonic sectors in the extended cage-net model.

Figure 4: Graphical representation of the local terms comprising the p-loop condensa- tion projector PE acting on a principal edge E. (a) A J-loop generated by the operator W J EV J E , creating a loop of four J-chargeons around the principal edge. (b) A J1 −J2 — cross generated by the operator term W J1 E V J2 E + W J2 E V J1 E . This configuration represents a symmetric superposition of two states where chargeons J1 and J2 are exchanged across the intersecting planes, ensuring the projector preserves cubic symmetry.

Figure 5: Illustration of certain p-loop configurations. The p-loop condensate is a super- position of various p-loop configurations fluctuating throughout the 3D lattice. Thick red loops represent the condensed p-loops formed along the principal edges, while the light red plaquettes indicate the underlying chargeon excitations that constitute these loops.

Figure 6: Restricted one-dimensional mobility of lineon. The thick red line denotes the strictly 1D linear subsystem along which the lineons are confined to move. Red plaquettes represent anyons carrying a ψ-flux, and the light red planes indicate the respective planes within which these ψ-fluxes reside. (a) A configuration where two lineons are localized on the principal edge E1. (b) The configuration after one of the lineons has hopped from edge E1 along the restricted 1D line to edge E3.
